channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Unlocking 2026: Beat Your Competition with AI Agents #ai #agents #aistrategy #llm #2026
executive_summary: In the video, Nate B Jones outlines five key AI trends that builders should focus on for 2026, emphasizing the importance of planning now amidst the rapidly changing AI landscape. He highlights how enforcement can create opportunities, CFOs are pushing for outcome-based pricing, AI-native companies are outpacing traditional firms, and local LLMs are crucial for market segmentation. Teams that invest in evaluations, multi-model resilience, and vertical expertise will be better positioned for success.
bullets: Enforcement is transforming governance into a builder’s market opportunity. This means that as regulations tighten, companies that can navigate these rules will find new avenues for growth. It's like finding a hidden path in a dense forest that leads to a treasure chest.

CFO scrutiny is driving a shift towards outcome-based pricing for AI agents. This means companies will pay based on the results they achieve rather than just for the technology itself. Think of it like paying a chef based on how delicious the meal is, rather than just for the ingredients.

AI-native entrants are outpacing incumbents in the market. This indicates that new companies built around AI are moving faster and more efficiently than established firms. It's like a sprinter who has been training specifically for a race versus a marathon runner who is used to a different pace.

Local LLMs (Large Language Models) are becoming essential for market segmentation. This means that companies need to tailor their AI solutions to specific local markets to be successful. Imagine a tailor who makes a suit that fits perfectly for one person but would be too loose for another.

Teams should invest now in evaluations, which means assessing the effectiveness of their AI tools. This proactive approach helps identify weaknesses before they become problems. It's like checking your car's oil regularly to avoid engine failure down the road.

Multi-model resilience is crucial for managing risk in AI development. This means using multiple models to ensure that if one fails, others can still deliver results. Think of it like having a backup generator; if the main power goes out, you still have light.

Focusing on agents in golden workflows is important for maximizing efficiency. Golden workflows are the most effective processes in an organization, and optimizing AI agents within them can yield significant benefits. It's like fine-tuning a race car to perform at its best on a specific track.

Vertical expertise is necessary for capturing the upside in 2026. This means having deep knowledge in specific industries to better tailor AI solutions. Imagine a doctor who specializes in one area of medicine, providing better care than a general practitioner.

The video suggests that teams should prioritize evaluations that check for code hygiene, with at least 50% of tests focusing on naming, structure, and function length. Currently, many teams have this split backwards at 80/20, which can lead to messy code. Think of it like cleaning your room; if you only tidy up the visible areas, the hidden mess will still cause problems.

Nate Jones emphasizes that planning for 2026 should start now, as the AI landscape is rapidly evolving. Delaying this planning could mean missing out on critical opportunities. It's like waiting until the last minute to book a flight; prices will only go up and availability will dwindle.

The video highlights that many builders and boards are getting confused by the constant hype surrounding AI. This uncertainty can lead to poor decision-making if teams don't stick to solid trends. Think of it like trying to navigate a ship in a storm without a compass; you need reliable guidance to reach your destination.

Jones mentions that he has pulled together insights from hundreds of sources, including conversations with industry insiders. This extensive research provides a solid foundation for the trends he discusses. It's like gathering a wealth of maps before embarking on a journey; the more information you have, the better prepared you are.
key_takeaways: Start planning for 2026 now by identifying key AI trends and aligning your strategy accordingly. This proactive approach will help you stay ahead of competitors who may be caught off guard by rapid changes. If you delay, you risk missing out on valuable opportunities that could set you apart.

Ensure that at least 50% of your evaluations focus on code hygiene, including naming, structure, and function length. This will help maintain clean and efficient code, which is essential for long-term success. If you ignore this, you may end up with a tangled mess that is hard to manage and fix.

Invest in multi-model resilience by developing multiple AI models that can support each other. This strategy will help mitigate risks and ensure that your systems remain operational even if one model fails. Without this investment, you may find yourself vulnerable to unexpected failures.

technical_terms.AI-native entrants: Companies that are built from the ground up to leverage artificial intelligence in their operations. Think of it like a plant that has been specifically bred to thrive in a certain environment, as opposed to one that is simply surviving in it.
technical_terms.Local LLMs: Large Language Models that are customized for specific local markets or languages. Like a local chef who knows the tastes and preferences of their community, these models provide more relevant and effective responses.
technical_terms.Outcome-based pricing: A pricing model where customers pay based on the results they achieve from a product or service rather than just the product itself. Think of it like paying for a successful advertising campaign rather than just the ad space.
technical_terms.Golden workflows: The most efficient and effective processes within an organization that yield the best results. Like a well-oiled machine, these workflows ensure that everything runs smoothly and efficiently.
technical_terms.Multi-model resilience: The strategy of using multiple AI models to ensure that if one fails, others can still deliver results. Think of it like having several lifeboats on a ship; if one fails, you still have others to rely on.
technical_terms.Code hygiene: The practice of maintaining clean and organized code to ensure it is easy to read, understand, and maintain. Like keeping your workspace tidy, good code hygiene prevents clutter and confusion.
technical_terms.CFO scrutiny: The increased attention and analysis from Chief Financial Officers regarding spending and investment decisions. Think of it like a coach reviewing game footage to make sure every play is effective and necessary.
technical_terms.Vertical expertise: Deep knowledge and specialization in a specific industry or sector. Like a master craftsman who knows every detail of their trade, vertical expertise allows for tailored solutions that meet unique needs.
technical_terms.Enforcement: The act of ensuring compliance with regulations and standards. Think of it like a referee in a game who makes sure all players follow the rules to maintain fairness.
technical_terms.Market segmentation: The process of dividing a market into distinct groups of consumers with similar needs or characteristics. Like slicing a pizza into different pieces, each segment can be targeted with specific strategies.
